planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer or early fall (August–September in the Northern Hemisphere) with the neck of the bulb at or just at soil surface in very well-drained, gritty, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Guernsey Lily is a South African native that flowers in autumn — the spectacularly iridescent, salmon-red to scarlet flowers appear on bare stems before the strap-like leaves emerge, creating a striking display in the fall garden.

directions: Plant bulbs in late summer with the neck at soil surface in very well-drained, gritty, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Nerine sarniensis requires sharp drainage and a summer dry rest period — withhold water almost entirely from May through August, then resume moderate watering when flower spikes emerge in autumn. Nerine sarniensis (Guernsey Lily) is a South African geophyte native to rocky, granitic slopes of the Cape Floristic Region whose name derives from an apocryphal story that a ship carrying the bulbs wrecked on the Channel Island of Guernsey in the 1650s, where the survivors naturalized and were first described by European botanists. The flowers are among the most iridescent of any bulb — the salmon-red to scarlet petals possess a unique crystalline cell-surface structure that scatters light, producing a glittering, almost metallic sheen in sunlight that has made them prized cut flowers since the 17th century. In the Cape fynbos ecology, the bulbs undergo a pronounced wet winter and dry summer cycle tied to winter-rainfall Mediterranean climate, which the cultivated plant reproduces best when kept dry from late spring through late summer. Naturalize in rock gardens, raised beds, or well-drained borders where the dry summer rest requirement is met; excellent cut flower lasting up to two weeks in a vase.
sources: Kirstenbosch National Botanical Garden — Cape Bulb Collection

planting.note: Plant container stock after last frost danger in well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in warm climates (zone 9–11); Wild Gardenia is a South African native woodland tree bearing intensely fragrant, pure-white 8-petaled flowers followed by hard, white, ribbed, woody fruits used traditionally as medicine and a source of yellow dye.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Gardenia thunbergia tolerates more sun than the common Gardenia jasminoides and is considerably more drought-tolerant once established — water deeply but infrequently, allowing the top few inches of soil to dry between irrigations. Gardenia thunbergia (Wild Gardenia or Woodland Gardenia) is a medium to large shrub or small tree native to the coastal forests and bushveld of South Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique, and Tanzania, differing from the familiar florist gardenia in producing strikingly symmetrical, 8-petaled, white waxy flowers — intensely fragrant in the evening — on a naturally sculptural, dark-barked tree that can reach 20–30 feet. The large, hard, ribbed woody fruits are distinctive — oval, white-grey, prominently ridged, and woody-shelled — and have historically been used as snuff containers, children's toys, and a source of yellow to gold fabric dye across southern Africa. Unlike Gardenia jasminoides, which suffers in alkaline soils, G. thunbergia is somewhat more tolerant of soil variation. It is prized in warm-climate gardens for its refined branching habit, glossy foliage, and exceptional evening fragrance, and makes an outstanding specimen tree or large fragrant hedge in zones 9–11.
sources: Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Gardenia thunbergia

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to loamy, near-neutral soil; Transvaal Gardenia is a deciduous southern and East African savanna tree adapted to seasonal drought and poor, sandy soils — it is more cold-tolerant than most gardenias, surviving light frosts in zone 9, and bears large, creamy-white, intensely fragrant flowers on bare branches before the new leaves flush in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; Gardenia volkensii is deciduous in seasonal dry conditions — water regularly during the growing season but allow a pronounced dry rest in winter, mimicking its native seasonal-rainfall savanna habitat. Gardenia volkensii (Transvaal Gardenia) is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to the bushveld, miombo woodland, and seasonal savanna of South Africa, Zimbabwe, Tanzania, Kenya, and East Africa to the Mozambique coast, where it produces spectacular, large, creamy-white, 6–8-petaled flowers with a powerful sweet fragrance on bare branches in late winter and early spring before leaf flush — making it one of the most dramatic late-winter flowering trees of the African dry savanna. The trees produce oval to oblong, distinctively ribbed, green to yellow fruits, nutritious to wildlife including elephants, baboons, and hornbills, and used medicinally across its range for skin conditions, fever, and eye ailments. It is hardier to cold than Gardenia thunbergia, tolerating brief periods at or just below freezing in dry winter conditions, making it suitable for protected spots in zone 9. Excellent drought-tolerant specimen or fragrant accent plant for hot, dry, sunny gardens.
sources: Trees of Southern Africa — Van Wyk and Van Wyk
Kenya Indigenous Forest Conservation Programme — Gardenia volkensii

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade in humid tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12); Needle Flower Tree (Posoqueria latifolia) is a Central and South American understory tree to rainforest edge shrub bearing spectacular clusters of long-tubed, pure-white, intensely fragrant, jasmine-like flowers — each flower tube 4–6 inches long — that open at dusk and are pollinated by hawk moths.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in partial shade to dappled light; Posoqueria latifolia benefits from consistent moisture and humidity — mulch heavily, water regularly, and protect from strong winds and salt spray. Posoqueria latifolia (Needle Flower Tree or Red Star) is an evergreen shrub to small tree native to humid tropical forests from southern Mexico through Central America and the Caribbean into South America, member of the Rubiaceae (coffee family), and one of the most ornamentally spectacular night-fragrant flowering trees of the tropics. The common name references the long, slender, needle-like flower tubes — each individual flower has a tube 4 to 6 inches long — held in large, globe-shaped terminal clusters that open white at night and release an intense jasmine-like fragrance evolved to attract long-tongued hawk moths (Sphingidae) as pollinators. The round, pale-yellow to orange fruits that follow are edible, mucilaginous, and eaten in some parts of its native range. Posoqueria makes an exceptional accent plant or large container specimen for humid subtropical and tropical gardens where it is a conversation-stopper in bloom; provide shelter, consistent moisture, and protection from frost for best results.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Tropical Ornamentals Collection

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, fertile, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 9–12); White Ixora (Ixora finlaysoniana) is a Southeast Asian native shrub bearing large, domed clusters of pure-white, 4-petaled, tubular fragrant flowers — far more fragrant than the common red Ixora coccinea — and is valued in Thai and Indian temple gardens for its pure white flower color and sweet honey-jasmine scent.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; White Ixora requires consistent moisture, good drainage, and acidic soil — iron chlorosis (yellowing leaves) occurs in alkaline soils and should be treated with acidified water and chelated iron supplements. Ixora finlaysoniana (White Ixora, Petch Lan Fah in Thai) is an evergreen shrub native to Myanmar, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, and Vietnam, grown throughout tropical Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Pacific for its large, rounded corymbs of pure-white, tubular, 4-petaled flowers held above glossy, dark-green foliage — and for its notably stronger, sweeter honey-jasmine fragrance compared to the familiar red-flowered Ixora coccinea of commerce. White Ixora carries deep cultural significance in Thai Buddhism, where the white flowers are offered at temples, shrines, and merit-making ceremonies, and in Hindu ritual use across South and Southeast Asia. Plants bloom continuously in warm, humid conditions and respond well to light pruning after each flush to encourage dense, compact growth and continuous flower production. Excellent for hedges, foundation plantings, and container specimens in subtropical and tropical gardens.
sources: Royal Forest Department Thailand — Ixora finlaysoniana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ixora in Florida Landscapes

planting.note: Plant in cool to mild climates (zone 8–10) in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade with support for climbing; Purple Apple Berry is a delicate Australian native twining vine from cool, moist forests of Victoria, Tasmania, and New South Wales bearing nodding, pale-yellow to greenish flowers followed by deep-purple, oblong berries eaten by Aboriginal Australians as bush tucker — one of few Australian native fruits suited to temperate and mild cool-temperate climates.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade with a trellis, fence, or shrub as support; Billardiera longiflora is a light, delicate twiner that does not require heavy support — it weaves through shrubs naturally and benefits from an organic mulch layer to keep the root zone cool and moist. Billardiera longiflora (Purple Apple Berry or Climbing Blueberry) is a dainty, evergreen, twining vine native to cool, moist forests and mountain gullies of Tasmania, Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales, where it climbs through the understory layer on fences and shrubs to produce small, nodding, narrowly bell-shaped, pale greenish-yellow to cream flowers in spring and early summer, followed in autumn by oblong, shiny, deep-purple berries roughly the size and shape of a large olive. The berries are entirely edible and were a prized bush food of southeastern Aboriginal peoples — sweet, slightly glutinous, with a mild, pleasant flavor reminiscent of a mild grape-berry combination. Billardiera longiflora is well-suited to cool-temperate and mild-temperate gardens in zones 8–10, thriving in sheltered, partially shaded positions in moist, well-drained, organically enriched soil. An excellent choice for cool-climate cottage gardens, shaded fences, and native wildlife gardens attracting insects and small birds.
sources: Australian National Botanic Gardens — Billardiera longiflora
